segments.title: Why the T.2 Dart suited carrier decks
segments.summary: The Blackburn T.2 Dart paired gentle low-speed handling with deck-landing features that made a large single-seat aircraft workable aboard carriers.
segments.body: Although large for a single-seat aircraft, the Dart is presented as a pilot-friendly machine with unusually manageable handling. Its nine-pound-per-square-foot wing loading and 38-knot stalling speed helped make carrier-deck landings less demanding. From 1928, reconditioned aircraft received Handley Page slots, adding another aid to low-speed approach control.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the Dart's handling, low wing loading, 38-knot stalling speed, carrier-deck landing qualities, and Handley Page slots.

segments.title: A dependable platform for torpedo technique
segments.summary: The Dart's ten years of rugged service gave the Fleet Air Arm a dependable aircraft for refining torpedo-dropping technique.
segments.body: The Dart was not a high-performance type, but the video stresses the value of its rugged dependability over a decade in service. That longevity mattered because it gave the Fleet Air Arm a stable aircraft with which to develop and perfect torpedo-dropping methods. It is a useful reminder that an air arm's tactical development can depend as much on reliable training platforms as on speed or headline performance.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts the Dart's modest performance with ten years of dependable service and its role in developing Fleet Air Arm torpedo-dropping techniques.

segments.title: Practising the low-level torpedo approach
segments.summary: At Gosport, a Dart-equipped torpedo training flight taught naval pilots the close-to-the-water approach needed for an attack run.
segments.body: The video describes D Torpedo Training Flight at Gosport teaching pilots to flatten out 15 feet above the water and aim the whole aircraft at the target. That detail conveys the demanding, deliberately low-level geometry of an early aerial-torpedo attack. Most Darts, however, normally served at sea with aircraft carriers rather than appearing in public-facing training demonstrations.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies D Torpedo Training Flight at Gosport, its 15-foot-low torpedo-approach instruction, and the Dart's normal carrier service.

segments.title: Making the Dart visible at air displays
segments.summary: A handful of early-1920s displays brought the carrier-borne Dart before the public, culminating in a five-aircraft mock torpedo attack from HMS Furious.
segments.body: Aircraft N9549 appeared at the Hendon RAF display in June 1923, while other Darts were shown at Croydon and the Prague international aero show. At the 1924 Hendon display, five Darts from HMS Furious demonstrated a mock torpedo attack on the dummy enemy ship Slavic before a large audience. These appearances offered a public demonstration of naval aviation at a time when most of the type's work was carried out at sea.

evidence.note: The transcript lists Dart appearances at Hendon, Croydon, and Prague, then describes five HMS Furious aircraft making a mock torpedo attack on the dummy ship Slavic at Hendon in 1924.
